coredata.dc:title: Sulphur-doped graphene quantum dot based fluorescent turn-on aptasensor for selective and ultrasensitive detection of omethoate
coredata.dc:creator: Nair, Raji V.
Chandran, Parvathy R.
Mohamed, A. Peer
Pillai, Saju
coredata.dc:description: Development of selective, ultra-sensitive, rapid and facile methods for the detection of chemical residues of toxic pesticides and hazardous chemicals are quite important in food safety, environmental monitoring and safeguarding public health. Herein, we presented a fluorescent turn-on aptasensor based on sulphur-doped graphene quantum dot (S-GQD) utilizing specific recognition and binding property of aptamer for the ultra-sensitive and selective detection of omethoate (OM) which is a systemic organophosphorus pesticide. The detection method is based on tuning aggregation-disaggregation mechanism of S-GQD by way of conformational alteration of the recognition probe. Fluorescence ‘turn-on’ process includes aggregation-induced quenching of S-GQD with aptamer via S-GQD-aptamer complex formation and its subsequent fluorescence recovery with the addition of OM by structural switching of S-GQD-aptamer complex to aptamer-omethoate complex. The reported ‘switch-on’ aptasensor has exhibited a low limit of detection of 0.001 ppm with high selectivity for OM over other pesticides.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aptasensor
Sulphur-doped graphene quantum dots
Fluorescence ‘turn-on’
Omethoate
Aggregation-disaggregation
Specific recognition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Aptamer based fluorescence detection of OM
Design and characterization of S-GQD-Apt sensing probe
Role of aptamer in ultrasensitive detection of OM

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thankfully acknowledge Mr. Kiran Mohan for TEM and Mr. Vishnu for AFM supports. Dr. Karunakaran is acknowledged for helping lifetime measurement. The authors acknowledge Dr. Reny Thankam Thomas (CSIR-NIIST) for productive discussions. R.V.N is thankful for CSIR (India) grant and P.R.C thanks SERB (India) for postdoctoral fellowship (PDF/2016/001839).

We thankfully acknowledge Mr. Kiran Mohan for TEM and Mr. Vishnu for AFM supports. Dr. Karunakaran is acknowledged for helping lifetime measurement. The authors acknowledge Dr. Reny Thankam Thomas (CSIR-NIIST) for productive discussions. R.V.N is thankful for CSIR (India) grant and P.R.C thanks SERB (India) for postdoctoral fellowship ( PDF/2016/001839 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis spectra (b) FTIR spectra of S-GQD, S-GQD-Apt complex and S-GQD-Rec.
XPS high-resolution spectra of (a) C 1s, (b) O 1s, (c) S 2p (d) N 1s and (e) P 2p of S-GQD-Apt complex, respectively.

(a) PL spectra of S-GQD, S-GQD-Apt complex and S-GQD-Rec. The inset shows the corresponding photographs (b) Time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) spectra of S-GQD, S-GQD-Apt complex and S-GQD-Rec.
TEM images of (a) S-GQD, (b) S-GQD-Apt complex, (c) S-GQD-Rec (Scale bar: 50 nm); and (d–f) corresponding magnified images (Scale bar: 20 nm).

PL spectra of (a) S-GQD in the presence of OM (0.001 ppm–200 ppm), (b) S-GQD in the presence of CF (0.001 ppm–200 ppm) (c) S-GQD-Apt complex in the presence of OM (0.001 ppm–200 ppm), (d) Linear regression plot with concentration of OM in the range of 0–200 ppm.

(a) PL spectra of S-GQD-Apt complex in the presence of CF (0.001 ppm–200 ppm), (b) Linear plot showing PL intensity of S-GQD and S-GQD-Apt complex in the presence of OM and CF. (c) Selectivity of the designed aptasensor for OM. (Concentrations of OM and other pesticides were optimised to 100 ppm).
Schematic illustration of a fluorescent ‘switch-on’ aptasensor using S-GQD-Apt complex for the selective detection of OM.
Performance comparison of various methods for the detection of OM.

head.ce:title: Sulphur-doped graphene quantum dot based fluorescent turn-on aptasensor for selective and ultrasensitive detection of omethoate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Functional Materials, Materials Science and Technology Division, CSIR-National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science and Technology (NIIST), Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala, 695 019, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR-National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science and Technology (NIIST)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Functional Materials, Materials Science and Technology Division, CSIR-National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science and Technology (NIIST), Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala, 695 019, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), Ghaziabad, Uttar Pradesh, 201 002, India

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), Ghaziabad, Uttar Pradesh, 201 002, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Functional Materials, Materials Science and Technology Division, CSIR-National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science and Technology (NIIST), Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala, 695 019, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR-National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science and Technology (NIIST)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of selective, ultra-sensitive, rapid and facile methods for the detection of chemical residues of toxic pesticides and hazardous chemicals are quite important in food safety, environmental monitoring and safeguarding public health. Herein, we presented a fluorescent turn-on aptasensor based on sulphur-doped graphene quantum dot (S-GQD) utilizing specific recognition and binding property of aptamer for the ultra-sensitive and selective detection of omethoate (OM) which is a systemic organophosphorus pesticide. The detection method is based on tuning aggregation-disaggregation mechanism of S-GQD by way of conformational alteration of the recognition probe. Fluorescence ‘turn-on’ process includes aggregation-induced quenching of S-GQD with aptamer via S-GQD-aptamer complex formation and its subsequent fluorescence recovery with the addition of OM by structural switching of S-GQD-aptamer complex to aptamer-omethoate complex. The reported ‘switch-on’ aptasensor has exhibited a low limit of detection of 0.001 ppm with high selectivity for OM over other pesticides.

ce:section.ce:para: In modern commercial and terrace farming agricultural practices, pesticides have become an inevitable component to boost crop productivity. However, the extensive use of pesticides can cause lethal effects to the environment and living things [1,2]. Thus the simple and rapid detection of pesticides residue at the levels prescribed by Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) remains as a challenging task for the research community [3]. Current methods for the detection of pesticides involve spectrophotometric and chromatographic techniques which are highly sensitive but laborious, time consuming, require sophisticated equipment and trained personnel. Therefore, researchers are attempting to design facile, low-cost sensors for the rapid detection of pesticide residues. Recent developments for detection of pesticides rely on development of biosensors which are highly sensitive, selective and can be miniaturized. A biosensor comprises of a biological recognition element that specifically interacts with a target molecule and a signalling component or transducer that converts the biological interactions into detectable signals. So far, various transducers including optical [4], calorimetric [5], acoustic [6] and electrochemical [7,8] based biosensors have been developed.

Though, numerous sensors have been reported with different recognition elements, antibodies and enzymes are among the most commonly used recognition elements as they have high selectivity and specificity [9,10]. However, high-temperature sensitiveness and easily denaturing nature of these recognition probes have limited their applications. Out of the reported recognition elements, aptamers are more selective, easily modifiable and stable [11,12]. Moreover, aptamers have a temperature tolerance up to 95 °C or exposed to organic solvents without showing considerable changes in functionality or structure [13]. The aptamers are referred to as single stranded small nucleic acid chains which can selectively bind target molecules. The target recognition results in folding of its primary sequence into characteristic three-dimensional (3D) structures. Even though aptamers are termed as chemical antibodies, they are proved to have a number of advantages over antibodies [14]. As antibodies are considered, the selection of a particular antibody requires a biological system and hence it is very difficult to develop an antibody against non-immunogenic targets and toxins. Therefore, aptamers are proved to be an alternative ligand for the development of new biosensor platforms. In recent times, several aptasensors have been developed adopting different sensing strategies like, electrochemical [15–17], optical [18], fluorescence [19], quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) [20] etc.

Till date, quite a lot of fluorescence based aptasensors have been realized using different fluorophores by taking the advantages of easiness in fabrication and operation, expedient signal transduction and quick response. Widely explored fluorophores are fluorescent dyes [21], metal quantum dots [22] and graphene quantum dots [23]. Compared with other inorganic quantum dots (QDs) like CdSe, InAs etc. and organic fluorescent dyes, the advantages of graphene quantum dots (GQD) are low cost, low toxicity and facile synthesis procedure [24]. Mostly the strategy adopted for the development of fluorescence based aptasensor is inner filter effect (IFE) which requires a fluorophore as well as an absorber [25]. Self-aggregation through non-covalent interactions such as hydrogen bonding interaction, electrostatic forces and pi–pi interaction followed by disaggregation trigged by analyte based switchable fluorescent aptasensor has been reported [26]. An aptamer-based fluorescent sensor has been reported by Li et al. for the detection of bisphenol-A mediated through the fluorescence quenching of CdTe QDs by IFE of AuNPs [27]. However, higher cytotoxicity and lower biocompatibility of metallic QDs have limited its use in biosensing. Recently, Wang and co-workers have designed an aptasensor based on IFE for the detection of acetamiprid pesticide [28]. However, IFE-based sensing approach results in low efficiency and poor sensitivity. In another report, Niu et al. has demonstrated a fluorescent turn-on sensor based on GQDs as fluorescent probe and AuNPs as emission quencher for Pb2+ employing fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [19]. However, FRET-based sensors have the disadvantage of giving false results because any external species can cause quenching of the fluorophore. Therefore, existing fluorescence-based studies prefer ‘turn-on’ sensors to FRET-based sensors. For now, only a few reports are available on pesticide detection through ‘turn-on’ principle using non-toxic QDs.

Herein, we demonstrated a simple design of ‘turn-on’ fluorescent aptasensor for the detection of OM. OM is a systemic organophosphorus insecticide used in agriculture and horticulture. Although, it is listed as a human carcinogen and as mutagenic, it is still widely used all over the world with the implementation of strict regulations by various monitoring agencies. The aptamer strategy was based on tuning the aggregation-disaggregation property of S-GQD for target specific sensing. Initially, aggregation induced quenching of S-GQD was mediated by its complex formation with aptamer. Eventually, highly sensitive detection of the pesticide OM was achieved by turning on fluorescence signal by structural switching of GQD-aptamer complex to aptamer-omethoate complex. High affinity and selective binding of aptamer to target molecule facilitated in realizing a simple, highly selective and sensitive aptasensor for OM.

Graphite (<150 μm), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), sulphuric acid (H2SO4), omethoate, carbofuran, methyl parathion, and thiram were procured from Sigma-Aldrich. Potassium permanganate (KMnO4) was procured from SD Fine-Chemicals Limited. Highly purified deionized water was used in all the experiments (Milli-Q D3, Merck, Germany). The oligonucleotides were procured from Eurofins Genomics. The base sequences are as follows:
5′-AAG CTT GCT TTA TAG CCT GCA GCG ATT CTT GAT CGG AAA AGG CTG AGA GCT ACG C-3′.
The characterization techniques used are discussed in supplementary material.

S-GQDs were synthesized as described in our previous report [29]. The procedure is described briefly as follows. S-GQDs were synthesized from GO with KMnO4 as an oxidizing agent and 0.1 M H2SO4 as a doping source for sulphur by microwave-assisted sonochemical method at 90 °C in 5 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aptamer based fluorescence detection of OM
ce:section.ce:para: S-GQD-aptamer complex (S-GQD-Apt) was prepared by mixing the obtained S-GQD (0.05 mg/mL) with 200 nM aptamer solution. The sensitive detection of OM was done using S-GQD-Apt complex in aqueous medium at RT. To examine the sensitivity towards OM, photoluminescence spectra (PL spectra) of S-GQD and S-GQD-Apt complex in the presence and absence of OM was recorded with excitation wavelength of 380 nm. For the detection, a series of concentrations of OM (0.001 ppm–200 ppm) were freshly prepared and added to S-GQD-Apt complex and kept it aside for the incubation time of 2 h. The subsequent fluorescence recovery was measured by recording the corresponding PL spectra at the same excitation wavelength. The selectivity towards OM was estimated by taking the PL spectra of S-GQD-Apt complex with some other pesticides such as carbofuran (CF), methyl parathion (MPr), and Thiram (Tr) of same concentration (100 ppm) under identical conditions.

100 μL of 300 ppm OM was mixed with S-GQD-Apt complex. The resultant mixture was vortex mixed so that OM and S-GQD-Apt complex can interact with each other efficiently. The S-GQD-Apt-OM mixture was then dialyzed in a 12 kDa dialysis bag against ultrapure deionized water for 24 h. The outer solution containing free S-GQD was replaced with ultrapure water every 4 h and stored. The collected outer solution was then concentrated to obtain S-GQD with concentration of 0.039 mg/mL which was about 78% of initial S-GQD taken and the recovered S-GQD labelled hereafter as, S-GQD-Rec.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and characterization of S-GQD-Apt sensing probe
ce:section.ce:para: S-GQDs having bright green fluorescence emission were synthesized from graphene oxide (GO) and H2SO4 (doping source). S-GQDs of uniform size distribution (∼4 nm), high crystallinity, excellent stability, monolayer graphene thickness, high quantum yield (27.8%) and production yield (85%) were synthesized [29]. Here we adopted a fluorescence sensing approach based on tuning of aggregation-disaggregation of S-GQDs for the detection of pesticide, OM. The feasibility of the proposed strategy was validated by treating S-GQDs with an aptamer (Apt), a specific recognition molecule for OM. The S-GQD-Apt complex (labelled as S-GQD-Apt) formed was employed as the sensor probe for the selective detection of OM.

The S-GQD and S-GQD-Apt complex were characterized using UV–Visible and FT-IR spectroscopy. The UV–Visible absorption spectra indicated the complexation of aptamer with S-GQD (Fig. 1 a). Here, in addition to the characteristic absorption peak of S-GQD at 380 nm, a peak at 260 nm was observed which corresponds to aptamer, indicating successful complexation of aptamer with S-GQD. From the FT-IR spectra, it was observed that both S-GQD and S-GQD-Apt complex exhibited characteristic peaks including broad bending vibration of –OH at 3340 cm−1, CO vibration peak at 1700 cm−1, CC stretching vibration peak at 1600 cm−1, SO at 1096 cm−1 and C–S stretching vibrations at 635 cm−1 (Fig. 1b). Besides, a few additional peaks at 1130 cm−1, 851 cm−1 and 640 cm−1 were also observed that correspond to symmetric phosphate, P–O and P–C, respectively in the case of S-GQD-Apt complex confirming the complexation of S-GQD with aptamer (Inset of Fig. 1a).

Further, XPS analysis was carried out to confirm the formation of S-GQD-Apt complex. The atomic percentage composition of the major elements in S-GQD, S-GQD-Apt complex and S-GQD-Rec are shown in Table S1. Fig. 2 (a–e) show the deconvoluted C 1s, O 1s, S 2p, N 1s and P 2p spectra of S-GQD-Apt complex. The C 1s peaks at 286.2 eV (N–CO), N 1s peak at 400.4 eV (HNCO), P 2p peaks at 132.1 eV and 133.5 eV (phosphate) confirmed the formation of S-GQD-Apt complex.

The PL spectra of S-GQD and S-GQD-Apt complex are shown in Fig. 3 a. Compared to S-GQD, S-GQD-Apt complex exhibited a significant fluorescence quenching caused by photo-induced electron transfer between S-GQD and aptamer. Therefore, S-GQD-Apt complex was employed as a detection probe for OM. Later, on adding 300 ppb solution of analyte OM, the fluorescence emission of S-GQD was restored. In the latter case, OM selectively binds to the aptamer of S-GQD-Apt complex to form Apt-OM complex resulting in the recovery of fluorescence of S-GQD. From the PL spectra, it was observed that the emission maximum of S-GQD-Rec was almost similar to that of S-GQD confirming the disaggregation of S-GQD. In order to understand the mechanism of the designed sensing strategy involving aggregation-disaggregation behaviour, fluorescence lifetime analysis was carried out by means of time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC). The emission lifetime of S-GQD, S-GQD-Apt complex and S-GQD-Rec were measured by monitoring the emission intensity as a function of decay time (Fig. 3b). The fluorescence lifetime of S-GQD was observed to be 3.3 ns which was higher than that of S-GQD-Apt complex (0.9 ns). Compared to S-GQD, the reduction in exciton lifetime of the complex could be due to the efficient exciton energy transfer from smaller S-GQD to larger aggregates in the complex. As a result, more rapid fluorescence emission decay was observed leading to the fluorescence quenching in S-GQD-Apt complex. Fluorescence recovery of S-GQD after the addition of analyte (OM) to S-GQD-Apt complex was again confirmed by the decay analysis. It was found that fluorescence lifetime of S-GQD-Rec is 2.9 ns which is almost similar to that of initial S-GQD (Table S2). The fluorescence lifetime results confirmed the viability of ‘switch-on’ aptasensor for the selective detection of OM.

Further, the aggregation-disaggregation profile of S-GQD in presence of both aptamer and OM was studied using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). The TEM images in Fig. 4 a and Fig. 4d shows that S-GQDs are evenly dispersed and possess uniform shape and narrow size distribution (∼4 nm). However, in the case of S-GQD-Apt complex, we could observe aggregated particles, indicating the aggregation of S-GQD in aptamer matrix (Fig. 4b and Fig. 4e). The addition of OM to S-GQD-Apt complex followed by purification instigated the larger aggregates be disappeared, the smaller S-GQDs of 4 nm sizes were recovered and uniformly distributed (Fig. 4c and Fig. 4f).

Circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopic analysis was carried out to further verify the detection method based on conformational change of aptamer (Fig. S1). Initially, we have established that there is no CD signal for OM. From the spectrum, it was observed that aptamer strand exhibited a positive peak at 235 and a negative peak at 225 indicating the typical single strand structure of aptamer due to its chiral properties. Upon the addition of OM, a distinct peak shift was observed with a positive peak at 230 and a negative band at 220 for Apt-OM complex. The peak shift observed for aptamer and the complex confirmed the switching of conformation of the aptamer strand, which can thus be utilized to tune aggregation-disaggregation behaviour of S-GQD for the detection of OM [30].

In the light of above discussions, a mechanism of fluorescence sensing for the detection of OM is put forwarded as illustrated in Scheme 1 . The mixing of aptamer with S-GQD resulted in the quenching of fluorescence owing to the aggregation of S-GQD in the matrix of oligonucleotide. Further, as expected, the addition of the analyte OM resulted in high fluorescence recovery which in turn can be ascribed to the selective binding of Apt and OM. The recognition and binding with OM persuaded conformational alteration in aptamer and has reversed the binding of S-GQD-Apt complex to Apt-OM complex with the discharge of fluorescent S-GQD. The selective binding induced S-GQD aggregation-disaggregation has led to the idea of developing a ‘switch-on’ aptasensor for pesticide.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of aptamer in ultrasensitive detection of OM
ce:section.ce:para: The role of aptamer in ultrasensitive detection of OM was studied by obtaining PL spectra of S-GQD with and without aptamer. The PL spectra of S-GQD with varying concentrations of two pesticides namely, carbofuran (CF) and OM without aptamer complexation showed aggregation induced fluorescence quenching. Fig. 5 a and Fig. 5b showed PL spectra of S-GQD with of CF and OM, respectively. It was observed that quenching occurred to same extent in both cases. Therefore, it was difficult to achieve selective detection towards a particular pesticide. In order to realize selective detection, we designed a ‘switch-on’ aptasensor by combining OM specific aptamer as a recognition probe with S-GQD to form S-GQD-Apt complex. The PL spectra of S-GQD-Apt complex showed significant fluorescence quenching with respect to S-GQD. To analyse the sensing performance of the designed fluorescent detection probe, OM of varying concentrations (0 ppm–200 ppm) was added to S-GQD-Apt complex and its PL spectra was measured upon an excitation wavelength at 380 nm. From the PL spectra (Fig. 5c), we found that the fluorescence emission intensity was enhanced progressively with the increase in concentration of OM. This is due to the reversal of S-GQD-Apt complex to very strong and unique Apt-OM complex and discharge of free and fluorescent S-GQD. Thus, OM triggered disaggregation of S-GQD-Apt complex resulted in fluorescence recovery of S-GQD. The fluorescence enhancement exhibited a good linear relationship with regression value, R2 of 0.9926 over the concentration range of 0–200 ppm (Fig. 5d). The developed ‘turn-on’ aptasensor has achieved a limit of detection at 0.001 ppm (1 ppb) for OM.

To demonstrate selective binding between aptamer and OM, the PL spectra of S-GQD-Apt complex in presence of analyte, CF was taken. From the spectra (Fig. 6 a), it is observed that addition of CF to S-GQD-Apt complex resulted in aggregation induced fluorescence quenching instead of fluorescence enhancement which suggests that the chosen aptamer sequence has no role in the detection of CF. The linear plot showing the fluorescence intensity of S-GQD with OM and CF in the presence and absence of aptamer compared fluorescence response of S-GQD with these pesticides and confirmed the role of aptamer in selective detection of OM (Fig. 6b). From the Figure, it is apparent that the PL intensity of OM in presence of S-GQD-Apt complex (S-GQD-Apt-OM) increases, while the others show a sharp decrease in the PL intensity. To evaluate the selectivity of the designed aptasensor, fluorescence response of S-GQD-Apt complex towards a set of pesticides such as OM, carbofuran (CF), Methyl parathion (MPr) and Thiram (Tr) of same concentration (100 ppm) was studied. Additionally, a mixture of these pesticides was taken to study the response of the aptasensor in a complex system of analytes. From Fig. 6c, it is clear that only OM exhibited a significant fluorescence recovery, while other analytes showed a decrease in the fluorescence intensity when compared to blank suggesting high selectivity towards OM. Further, a significant fluorescence recovery of 51% was observed for the mixture of pesticides as well. As compared to the sensing of omethoate alone, the reduction in fluorescence intensity of the mixture may be due to the quenching effect of other pesticides present in the mixture. These results confirmed that the designed ‘switch-on’ aptasensor is selective for the detection of OM and the system is competent to detect OM even in complex samples. The selective detection of OM is due to the specific recognition and strong affinity of aptamer towards OM.

Performance comparison of this method with other OM detection methods has been tabulated in Table 1
. From Table 1, it could be seen that compared with most of the detection methods the present work reported a very low LOD of 1 ppb (4.7 nM). Although Zhang et al. reported better LOD, they used graphene oxide as an additional fluorescence quencher to nitrogen-doped GQD-Apt probe for the fluorescence detection of OM [31]. However, our detection strategy used S-GQD complexed with the aptamer alone as the detection probe for OM. Moreover, the method has good selectivity and wider detection range.

In summary, we demonstrated a facile and efficient fluorescent ‘turn-on’ aptasensor based on specific recognition of aptamer for the ultrasensitive and selective detection of OM. The aggregation-disaggregation profile of S-GQDs induced by the structural switching of aptamer led to fluorescence quenching and fluorescence recovery, respectively. The developed ‘turn-on’ aptasensor demonstrates a very high selectivity towards OM over other control pesticides. The sensor has realized an efficient quantitative detection of OM with LOD as low as 1 ppb. The proposed fluorescent ‘turn-on’ aptasensor does not require any further alteration of aptamer sequence or signal transmitting nanoparticle which makes it facile to design a simple and efficient fluorescent turn-on aptasensor for OM detection. In addition, the developed sensor exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity towards OM, even in mixture of analytes (complex sample), due to selective binding and high affinity of aptamer sequence to the target molecule. Moreover, the fluorescent signal material used can be recovered for further usage. Considering all the above advantages, we conclude that the proposed aptamer strategy can offer alternative approaches for other targets of interest in the area of food safety and environmental monitoring.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thankfully acknowledge Mr. Kiran Mohan for TEM and Mr. Vishnu for AFM supports. Dr. Karunakaran is acknowledged for helping lifetime measurement. The authors acknowledge Dr. Reny Thankam Thomas (CSIR-NIIST) for productive discussions. R.V.N is thankful for CSIR (India) grant and P.R.C thanks SERB (India) for postdoctoral fellowship (PDF/2016/001839).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2021.338893.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Exposures of children to organophosphate pesticides and their potential adverse health effects

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Eskenazi, A. Bradman, R. Castorina, Exposures of children to organophosphate pesticides and their potential adverse health effects, Environmental health perspectives 107(suppl 3) (1999) 409-419.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Adverse effects of pesticides and related chemicals on enzyme and hormone systems of fish, amphibians and reptiles: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.Z. Khan, F.C. Law, Adverse effects of pesticides and related chemicals on enzyme and hormone systems of fish, amphibians and reptiles: a review, Proceedings of the Pakistan Academy of Sciences 42(4) (2005) 315-323.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Environmental Protection Agency and other methods for the determination of priority pesticides and their transformation products in water

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Barcelo, Environmental Protection Agency and other methods for the determination of priority pesticides and their transformation products in water, Journal of Chromatography A 643(1-2) (1993) 117-143.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanoparticle-based optical biosensors for the direct detection of organophosphate chemical warfare agents and pesticides

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Simonian, T. Good, S.-S. Wang, J. Wild, Nanoparticle-based optical biosensors for the direct detection of organophosphate chemical warfare agents and pesticides, Analytica chimica acta 534(1) (2005) 69-77.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticle peroxidase mimetic-based colorimetric assay for the rapid detection of organophosphorus pesticide and nerve agent

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Liang, K. Fan, Y. Pan, H. Jiang, F. Wang, D. Yang, D. Lu, J. Feng, J. Zhao, L. Yang, Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticle peroxidase mimetic-based colorimetric assay for the rapid detection of organophosphorus pesticide and nerve agent, Analytical chemistry 85(1) (2012) 308-312.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Label-free immunosensor based on micromachined bulk acoustic resonator for the detection of trace pesticide residues

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wang, D. Chen, Y. Xu, W. Liu, Label-free immunosensor based on micromachined bulk acoustic resonator for the detection of trace pesticide residues, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical 190 (2014) 378-383.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Origami multiple paper-based electrochemical biosensors for pesticide detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Arduini, S. Cinti, V. Caratelli, L. Amendola, G. Palleschi, D. Moscone, Origami multiple paper-based electrochemical biosensors for pesticide detection, Biosensors and Bioelectronics 126 (2019) 346-354.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical aptasensor based on one step co-electrodeposition of aptamer and GO-CuNPs nanocomposite for organophosphorus pesticide detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Fu, X. An, Y. Yao, Y. Guo, X. Sun, Electrochemical aptasensor based on one step co-electrodeposition of aptamer and GO-CuNPs nanocomposite for organophosphorus pesticide detection, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical 287 (2019) 503-509.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibody-based immobilization of bioluminescent bacterial sensor cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.R. Premkumar, O. Lev, R.S. Marks, B. Polyak, R. Rosen, S. Belkin, Antibody-based immobilization of bioluminescent bacterial sensor cells, Talanta 55(5) (2001) 1029-1038.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multicolor sensor for organophosphorus pesticides determination based on the bi-enzyme catalytic etching of gold nanorods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, B. Lv, A. Liu, G. Liang, L. Yin, Y. Pu, W. Wei, S. Gou, S. Liu, Multicolor sensor for organophosphorus pesticides determination based on the bi-enzyme catalytic etching of gold nanorods, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical 265 (2018) 675-681.
